statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
You are given two integer arrays $ a $ and $ b $ both of length $ n $ .

You can choose any subset of indices and swap the elements at those positions (i. e. make a swap( $ a_i $ , $ b_i $ ) for each $ i $ in the subset). A subset of indices is considered good if, after the swapping, both arrays are sorted in non-descending order.

Your task is to calculate the number of good subsets. Since the answer can be large, print it modulo $ 998244353 $ .

Input Format
The first line contains a single integer $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 500 $ ) — the number of test cases.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer $ n $ ( $ 1 \le n \le 100 $ ).

The second line contains $ n $ integers $ a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n $ ( $ 1 \le a_i \le 1000 $ ).

The third line contains $ n $ integers $ b_1, b_2, \dots, b_n $ ( $ 1 \le b_i \le 1000 $ ).

Additional constraint on the input: there is at least one good subset.

Output Format
For each test case, print a single integer — the number of good subsets, taken modulo $ 998244353 $ .

Examples
Sample 1
Input
3
3
2 1 4
1 3 2
1
4
4
5
2 3 3 4 4
1 1 3 5 6
Output
2
2
8

Note
In the first example, there are $ 2 $ good subsets: {1, 3} and {2}.

In the second example, there are $ 2 $ good subsets: {1} and {}.

In the third example, there are $ 8 $ good subsets: {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, {1, 2, 3}, {1, 2, 4, 5}, {1, 2}, {3, 4, 5}, {3}, {4, 5} and {}.

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2144C - Non-Descending Arrays
First, let's understand how we can always obtain at least one subset. Let's keep the maximum elements from all pairs in one array, and the minimums in another (that is, if $$$a_i  \gt  b_i$$$, we swap them, so that $$$a$$$ will contain the minimums from each pair, and $$$b$$$ will contain the maximums).
We can prove that if there is at least one way to make both arrays sorted, then this way will definitely work. To do this, let's assume that we have reached a situation where one of the arrays is not sorted using this method:
if in array $$$a$$$ we have a pair of elements $$$a_i  \gt  a_{i+1}$$$, then considering that $$$a_i \le b_i$$$, it follows that $$$b_i  \gt  a_{i+1}$$$. This means that whichever of the two elements $$$(a_i, b_i)$$$ we place before $$$a_{i+1}$$$, it will definitely be greater;
if in array $$$b$$$ we have a pair of elements $$$b_i  \gt  b_{i+1}$$$, then considering that $$$a_{i+1} \le b_{i+1}$$$, it follows that $$$b_i  \gt  a_{i+1}$$$. This means that whichever of the two elements $$$(a_{i+1}, b_{i+1})$$$ we place after $$$b_i$$$, it will definitely be smaller.
Thus, if at least one good subset exists, we can always ensure that $$$a_i \le b_i$$$. Let's initially swap the elements in those pairs where $$$a_i  \gt  b_i$$$; the answer will not change (since the $$$2^n$$$ subsets of indices we are considering will yield the same results after such a transformation, just in a different order).
Now let's understand which pairs of elements we can swap while keeping the arrays sorted. For each pair of adjacent indices $$$(i, i+1)$$$, there are two possible situations:
either $$$b_i \le a_{i+1}$$$, in which case any element at position $$$i$$$ will be less than or equal to any element at position $$$i+1$$$ (let's call this a type $$$1$$$ index pair);
or $$$b_i  \gt  a_{i+1}$$$, in which case we must place $$$a_{i+1}$$$ after $$$a_i$$$ and $$$b_{i+1}$$$ after $$$b_i$$$. This means we should swap $$$a_i$$$ and $$$b_i$$$ if and only if we swap $$$a_{i+1}$$$ and $$$b_{i+1}$$$ (let's call this a type $$$2$$$ index pair).
Let's consider the "segments" of indices $$$i$$$ where every two adjacent indices form a type $$$2$$$ pair. Then if we choose what to do with any one index in this pair, it will automatically force us to choose an action for all other indices in this segment. However, different segments do not affect each other, as the indices at the boundary of two segments form a type $$$1$$$ pair.
Thus, the answer is $$$2^{m}$$$, where $$$m$$$ is the number of segments of type $$$2$$$ pairs. Each type $$$2$$$ pair "merges" two such segments, so we can calculate the answer as $$$2^{n-k}$$$, where $$$k$$$ is the number of such $$$i$$$ such that $$$b_i  \gt  a_{i+1}$$$ (after we have swapped $$$a_i$$$ and $$$b_i$$$ in pairs where $$$a_i  \gt  b_i$$$).

if

[

-

(

)
%

;
}

<<

<<
'\n'
;
